posts.title: Die Hard Arcade
posts.alt: Die Hard Arcade screenshot: a beat-em-up brawl in a concrete parking garage, the white-shirted hero in green pants at the right fighting three yellow-suited firefighter enemies, one airborne mid-kick and one knocked flat on the teal tiled floor, a red fire truck parked behind, a dropped red handgun on the ground, a FIRE AXE item panel at the lower left and PRESS START in green across the top
body: Sega's 1996 arcade beat-em-up Die Hard Arcade is a Die Hard movie tie-in in the West, where players "play as either John McClane or Kris Thompsen". In Japan, though: "Because Sega did not hold the Japanese video game rights to the franchise, the game was stripped of the Die Hard license in Japan and published as an original property" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Atari Lynx
posts.alt: Atari Lynx screenshot: the BMX event from California Games, a rider in blue cresting a brown dirt mound on a bike, chunky green grass blocks lining the track, a red 0:27 timer at the top left and the score 13127 at the top right, in the handheld's bright color graphics
body: Atari's 1989 Lynx handheld connects units for multiplayer over a ComLynx cable, but that connection was nearly wireless: "The networking system was originally developed to run over infrared links and codenamed RedEye. This was changed to a cable-based networking system before the final release as the infrared beam was too easily interrupted when players walked through the beam, according to Peter Engelbrite" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Star Soldier: Vanishing Earth
posts.alt: Star Soldier: Vanishing Earth screenshot: the player's small red ship at the bottom of a purple metallic canyon firing upward at green winged enemies and an exploding foe, a health bar reading MID and the number 189 in the top-left HUD, Ex LV 1-2 and SC 851680 across the top right
body: Star Soldier: Vanishing Earth is Hudson Soft's 1998 shooter, part of the Star Soldier series. "The Star Force series, along with Hudson's Star Soldier, was often featured in the popular Japanese gaming tournament known as "Hudson All-Japan Caravan Festival". Home ports of Star Soldier games would sometimes feature "Caravan" modes in which the player would race through timed stages while trying to accumulate a high score." (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Salamander
posts.alt: Salamander screenshot: the player's small Vic Viper ship at the bottom facing a large red organic sphere boss webbed with glowing yellow cracks, mechanical gray wall structures down both sides, the score 693100 and HI 693100 across the top and GAME OVER in the upper right, all on a dark background
body: Konami's 1986 shooter Salamander was renamed Life Force in North America, where "the American version changes the game's plot by adding an opening text that establishes the game to be set inside a giant alien life-form which is infected by a strain of bacteria" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Tower & Shaft
posts.alt: Tower & Shaft mode-select screen for the Tower half: the orange TOWER logo on a wooden vine-framed panel at the upper left, a red menu banner and a green menu banner with Japanese text down the right side, the line CANCEL : START BUTTON and a large yellow countdown 19 at the lower left, CREDIT 0 in the bottom-right corner
body: Aruze's 2003 Tower & Shaft is "an arcade game based on both NS-Tower and NS-Shaft", two shareware Macintosh games from Nagi-P Software, "a Japanese software company founded in 1989 by Akihiko Kusanagi at the age of 13" (per HandWiki).

posts.title: Eleven Beat
posts.alt: Eleven Beat matchup screen: the heading TOURNAMENT 1ST ROUND over a packed stadium crowd, COM Japan on the left and 1P Brazil on the right, a yellow VS between them, and the large national flags of Japan and Brazil below on the pitch, CREDIT(S) 0 at the lower right
body: Eleven Beat, a Hudson Soft arcade game, ran on Seta's Aleck 64, a board that "essentially consists of a Nintendo 64 board retrofitted with sound capabilities that were standard for arcade games of the time." It was "designed by SETA Corporation in cooperation with Nintendo", yet "Nintendo did not release any arcade game using this hardware" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Tempest
posts.alt: Tempest screenshot: the vector playfield, a circular web of thin blue lanes radiating out from a small dark hole at the center, the yellow claw-shaped blaster ship at the bottom rim near a few small enemies by the hole, GAME OVER in green across the top, all on a black background
body: Atari's 1981 arcade game Tempest is a tube shooter, and its designer, Dave Theurer, says the design "came from a nightmare where monsters crawled out of a hole in the ground". From there, he "basically just took Space Invaders and wrapped the surface into a circle" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Magical Tetris Challenge
posts.alt: Magical Tetris Challenge character select screen: a wooden Character Select sign hangs over a sunlit village plaza, the four playable Disney characters standing in a row beneath it, Mickey Mouse, Donald Duck, Goofy and Minnie Mouse, each labeled by name, with CREDIT 0 counters along the bottom
body: Capcom's 1998 arcade game Magical Tetris Challenge is Tetris "featuring Disney characters". "The last three stages pit the player's character against a Weasel and the Big Bad Wolf before facing the game's main antagonist, Pete" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Arkanoid
posts.alt: Arkanoid ending screen: a large detailed starship in blue and red set against a black starfield, red text across the top reading BUT THE REAL VOYAGE OF ARKANOID IN THE GALAXY HAS ONLY STARTED, with the HUD line 1UP 15000 and HIGH SCORE 50000 above it
body: Taito's 1986 arcade block breaker: "Arkanoid was part of a contest within Taito, where two teams of designers had to implement a block breaker game and determine which was superior to the other" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Wonder Boy
posts.alt: Wonder Boy screenshot: a boss encounter in a blue brick chamber with two ornate stone columns, the small blonde caveman boy at the lower left approaching a large seated antagonist in blue at the lower right, a light brick floor along the bottom, an item and food bar and the score PLAYER1 85210 with HIGH 85210 across the top
body: Sega's 1986 arcade platformer Wonder Boy was developed by Escape. "Escape had a licensing arrangement whereby it owned the rights to the game design, but Sega retained the Wonder Boy trademark." "To get around the licensing issue, Hudson Soft had the title changed which resulted into Adventure Island. Although Hudson could have featured the generic boy from Wonder Boy as the protagonist of Adventure Island, it instead created a brand new character, Master Higgins" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Rygar
posts.alt: Rygar high score screen: a yellow BEST 5 banner over four red column headings RANK, SCORE, NAME and ROUND, five ranked rows each reading 30000 with the initials AAA and ROUND 02, the score 10730 and HI-SCORE 30000 in the top HUD, and ROUND-01 in yellow at the lower right, all on a black background
body: Rygar is Tecmo's 1986 arcade platformer, named for its hero. Per Wikipedia, "the Roman syllables "Ry" and "Li" both come from the same Japanese character, meaning there is no differentiation between "L" and "R" sounds. Due to this ambiguity, when the game was translated into English, the villain retained the name Ligar, but the hero was mistakenly called Rygar."

posts.title: Rolling Thunder
posts.alt: Rolling Thunder high score screen: a HIGH SCORE heading with 1UP and 2UP counters, a TOP 5 table listing SCORE, AREA and INI'T columns with five entries from 30000 down to 8000, all set over a detailed gray control-room console of monitors, dials and colored panels, CREDIT 0 at the bottom
body: Namco's 1986 arcade game Rolling Thunder is a run-and-gun. Ryan Lambie, writing for Den of Geek, considers it "the precursor to the modern cover shooter" for the way "the player can hide behind crates, doors and other obstacles to avoid enemy fire" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: X-Men vs. Street Fighter
posts.alt: X-Men vs. Street Fighter versus screen: the mutant Rogue in green with white-streaked hair on the left, Street Fighter's Ryu in his white gi on the right, a large pink VS between the two teams, the team names ROGUE and CYCLOPS at the lower left and RYU and WOLVERINE at the lower right
body: Capcom's 1996 arcade game X-Men vs. Street Fighter is a tag-team fighter: each player picks two characters and switches between them mid-fight. Of the home port: "due to the memory limitations of the PlayStation ... the tag team combat was omitted" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Out Run
posts.alt: Out Run screenshot: the music-select screen, the heading SELECT MUSIC BY STEERING over the track name MAGICAL SOUND SHOWER, above a first-person dashboard view with a hand on the wheel and a lit radio readout, a palm tree at the left and turquoise sea ahead under a blue sky
body: Sega's 1986 arcade racer Out Run gave players "a radio with soundtrack selection". Per Wikipedia, "the game's selectable radio music has become commonplace in video games such as the Grand Theft Auto series".

posts.title: Sea Wolf
posts.alt: Sea Wolf screenshot: white-on-black submarine game, a cartoon ZAP! explosion bursting mid-water as a torpedo hits, a ship crossing the wavy surface line above, TIME 43 and SCORE 800 at the bottom
body: "In the United States, Sea Wolf was the highest-grossing arcade video game for two years in a row: 1976 and 1977." And: "Sea Wolf is one of the first video games to include a high score feature, saving the highest value achieved prior to powering off" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Renegade
posts.alt: Renegade high score screen: a red BEST SCORE heading over a five-row ranking table, the initials GLF, PRM, LDS, ABG and JIM beside scores from 50000 down to 20000, all set against a gray brick wall topped with barbed wire, a stack of yellow crates at the left and a red fire hydrant at the lower left
body: "Conceptualized and designed by Yoshihisa Kishimoto, the game was semi-autobiographical, partly based on his own teenage high school years getting into daily fights, with Kunio partly based on himself" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Fairchild Channel F
posts.alt: Maze screenshot on the Fairchild Channel F: a blue rectangular maze of interconnected passages filling a gray playfield, rendered in the console's coarse low-resolution graphics
body: Chipmaker Fairchild's Channel F, released in 1976, was the first console "to be based on a microprocessor and to use ROM cartridges" rather than games built into the box (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Atari Jaguar
posts.alt: Tempest 2000 screenshot: a glowing blue geometric web tunnel receding into a black starfield, a yellow claw-shaped player ship gripping the near rim, and the score 150 with three life icons in the top-left corner
body: Console maker Atari released the Jaguar home console in 1993. "Atari tried to downplay competing consoles by proclaiming the Jaguar was the only "64-bit" system; in its marketing in the American market the company used the tagline "Do the math!", in reference to the 64 number. This claim was widely derided as misleading, because the Motorola 68000 CPU and the Tom and Jerry coprocessors execute 32-bit instruction sets." Electronic Gaming Monthly ran a mini-editorial: "If Sega did the math for the Sega Saturn the way Atari did the math for their 64-bit Jaguar system, the Sega Saturn would be a 112-bit monster of a machine" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Gulf War II
posts.alt: Gulf War II high score screen: a BEST 20 ranking table with RANK, NAME, RND and SCORE columns over a dark blue background, every entry the placeholder initials CCC, the blue COMAD logo near the bottom above the line COMAD CORP.1991
body: Gulf War II is a 1991 vertical arcade shooter by game maker Comad, a studio in Seoul, South Korea. It is not entirely Comad's own work: the game is "a very extensive hack of Twin Cobra", the 1987 coin-op by shooter studio Toaplan, "although Toaplan wasn't credited in-game". The game is titled Gulf War II, but "there was never a Gulf War I" (per BootlegGames Wiki).

posts.title: Run and Gun
posts.alt: Run and Gun screenshot: the pre-game TONIGHT'S MATCH-UP screen, the team name SAN-ANTONIO and its badge above a central VS emblem, NEW YORK and its blue badge below, all over a wooden basketball court
body: Of the cabinet behind Konami's 1993 arcade game Run and Gun, known as Slam Dunk in Japan: "The cabinet is a unique design with dual monitors showing opposite ends of the court in proper perspective" (per the Museum of the Game).

posts.title: Act-Fancer Cybernetick Hyper Weapon
posts.alt: Act-Fancer screenshot: a boss fight, the small silver-armored player creature standing at lower left as a large pale grotesque boss looms on the right, its long neck and clawed limbs reaching out over red organic matter, a ruined city skyline behind under a blue sky
body: Data East's 1989 arcade run-and-gun Act-Fancer has a lineage its title never advertises: "the goofily named Act-Fancer is actually a spinoff of the Darwin series", the maker's vertical shooters. Both share an evolution system: power up and the creature you control morphs into stronger forms, but the progress is fragile, since "a single hit will take you back to the weakest, almost useless form, and you still need to grab orbs to keep from devolving", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: Hotdog Storm
posts.alt: Hotdog Storm high score screen: the game's logo across the top over a ten-row score table with red ranks, blue scores, green initials and white round numbers, all laid over a large emblem of a hot dog curled into a ring around a central star badge reading SUPERSONICS, with small hot dogs scattered around the border
body: In game maker Marble's 1996 vertical shooter Hotdog Storm, food appears only on the high score screen, "which features flying hot dogs and condiment bottles", writes SCROLL.

posts.title: Mazinger Z
posts.alt: Mazinger Z screenshot: a vertical-shooter boss fight, the player's small red and white robot at the bottom firing up at a large golden robot boss with green clawed side-pods that rains orange fireballs down the screen, a pilot portrait and energy gauge in the top-left HUD reading GRENDIZER
body: Banpresto's 1994 arcade game Mazinger Z is a vertical shooter based on the manga of the same name by Go Nagai, and it stayed a Japan-only coin-op until Hamster Corporation reissued it for the Arcade Archives line in 2023. "It was the first game in the series to be based on a non-game property; due to the involvement of Nagai himself, Toei Animation and Banpresto owner Bandai Namco Entertainment, Hamster sold the game at double the series' usual price to pay the license fees" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Rampage
posts.alt: Rampage screenshot: the attract mode's top-scores screen, INSERT COINS and TODAYS TOP SCORES over three scores up top, and below them portraits of the three snarling monsters with their names: the brown gorilla George at left, the green reptile Lizzie at center, the gray werewolf Ralph at right
body: The three playable monsters in Bally Midway's 1986 arcade game Rampage began as people: "George, a scientist who transformed into a King Kong-like gorilla due to the influence of experimental vitamins; Lizzie, a woman who transformed into a Godzilla-like reptile after swimming in a lake contaminated with radioactive waste; and Ralph, who transformed into a gigantic werewolf after unwittingly eating a hot dog with an unknown food additive" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Star Wars
posts.alt: Star Wars screenshot: the first-person space dogfight, blue and red wireframe TIE fighters and laser fire against a starfield, a burst of colorful explosions in the center, the red X-wing cockpit frame at the four corners, a shield meter and the words SHOOT FIREBALLS across the top
body: Of one marathon on Atari's 1983 arcade game Star Wars: "In June 1985, Flavio Tozzi, Dave Roberts and Mike Ohren played as a team in turns for five days, two hours and 26 minutes on a single credit to attain the world record score of 1,000,000,012 points." "Their efforts raised money for a local charity" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Blood Bros.
posts.alt: Blood Bros. screenshot: the blue-shirted cowboy hero at lower right firing at a row of enemy horsemen charging across a grassy field, flames at his feet and a log fence in front, a fort wall and green trees behind
body: Blood Bros. is a 1990 arcade shooter by TAD Corporation, a Wild West follow-up to the studio's own Cabal. "Player one is a cowboy and player two is a Native American", and "the goofy factor has been raised significantly higher, with your most likely source of munitions of all things being pigs": "Red ones give you dynamite, black ones drop shotguns, and pink ones dispense the heavy machine gun", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: Caveman Ninja
posts.alt: Caveman Ninja screenshot: one of the cavemen on a rocky ledge below the gaping head of a giant tyrannosaur, as a pterodactyl flies off carrying a captured cavewoman, a volcano behind
body: Data East's 1991 arcade platformer Caveman Ninja stars "the green-haired Joe and the blue-haired Mac, cavemen who battle through numerous prehistoric levels". Its Japanese version opens with "a beginning scene in which cavemen enter a hut and emerge while dragging cavewomen by their hair." That scene "was removed from the US release", and Data East explained the cut: "We didn't want kids to see [the Japanese display] and think it was okay" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Warrior Blade
posts.alt: Warrior Blade screenshot: the barbarian Rastan, sword raised, facing a tall gray winged demon boss with a long tail on a red-tiled temple floor under a dark red sky
body: Warrior Blade - Rastan Saga Episode III is Taito's 1991 arcade beat-em-up, the third Rastan game. Its player character, Rastan, fights with the help of a magical ally, "an ancient wizard named Mahadidekaradi", whose screen-clearing spells each come with "a lengthy text incantation that flashes on the screen as your enemies are destroyed." His foes take after the Conan stories of author Robert E. Howard: "the bosses appear to be unstoppable demons, but all crumble and die very anti-climatically, with just a single scream as they become lifeless and fall apart", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: Legionnaire
posts.alt: Legionnaire screenshot: the blue-clad fighter Alfred facing a cluster of red-uniformed Crimson Gang enemies on a girder bridge, a city skyline behind them
body: Legionnaire is a 1992 arcade beat-em-up by TAD Corporation, the studio "mostly known for the gallery shooter Cabal". It "once had a reputation as a lost game": its copy protection "prevented it from being properly emulated in MAME", and so "it was basically forgotten until the MAME team decrypted it," writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: The Ninja Warriors
posts.alt: The Ninja Warriors screenshot: the red and blue player ninjas fighting teal claw-wielding enemies on a street outside a building marked Library, a statue at right
body: The Ninja Warriors is Taito's 1987 arcade side-scroller. "Since the ninjas are technically robots, they take battle damage. If you take too many blows to the head, your mask and face peel away to reveal a cyborg skull similar to The Terminator," writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: Captain America and The Avengers
posts.alt: Captain America and The Avengers screenshot: the Mech Taco boss, a red robotic octopus with a screen for a face and a long segmented tentacle arm, its name on the boss bar
body: Data East's 1991 Marvel arcade beat-em-up sends the Avengers through occasional flight stages. Of the bosses there, writes Hardcore Gaming 101, "Amongst the most amusing of these is a fight against a robotic octopus called" Mech Taco. "Its goofy name obviously has nothing to do with the Mexican food, but rather that" tako "means" octopus "in Japanese, a rather hilarious language flub in a game featuring some overwhelmingly American action heroes".

posts.title: Metamoqester
posts.alt: Metamoqester screenshot: a fighter attacking a giant blue crystalline golem on a snowy stage as penguins look on
body: Metamoqester is Banpresto's 1995 arcade fighter. It was "originally released in Japan in November 1995" as the "pronounceable" Oni - The Ninja Master. Of the name it carries everywhere else, "why/how they came up with the name they did is still a mystery", writes Hardcore Gaming 101.

posts.title: Apple IIGS
posts.alt: Apple IIGS GS/OS desktop: the System folder window open next to an Info on Finder window listing the Finder's authors
body: The "GS" in the name stands for "Graphics and Sound". On the sound half of that: "The Apple IIGS's sound is provided by an Ensoniq 5503 DOC (Digital Oscillator Chip) wavetable synthesis chip designed by Bob Yannes, creator of the SID synthesizer chip used in the Commodore 64." Of that chip: "The inclusion of a professional-grade sound chip in the Apple IIGS was hailed by both developers and users, and hopes were high that it would be added to the Macintosh; however, it drew another lawsuit from Apple Corps." "As part of an earlier trademark dispute with the business arm of The Beatles, Apple Computer had agreed not to release music-related products" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Apple Lisa
posts.alt: Apple Lisa Office System screenshot: the File/Print menu pulled down over a window of document icons, with LisaGraph and LisaCalc papers on the desktop and a trash can in the corner
body: Apple's 1983 Lisa "was the first mass-market personal computer operable through a graphical user interface (GUI)". Officially, Apple "stated that the name was an acronym for 'Local Integrated Software Architecture'". But "because Steve Jobs's first daughter was named Lisa (born in 1978), it was sometimes inferred that the name also had a personal association". Apple software developer Andy Hertzfeld said the acronym "was reverse-engineered from the name 'Lisa' in late 1982 by the Apple marketing team". "Privately, Hertzfeld and the other software developers used 'Lisa: Invented Stupid Acronym', a recursive backronym, and computer industry pundits coined the term 'Let's Invent Some Acronym' to fit Lisa's name" (per Wikipedia). Decades later, Jobs told his biographer Walter Isaacson: "Obviously it was named for my daughter."

posts.title: Boogie Wings
posts.alt: Boogie Wings screenshot: a player-controlled tank firing up at a giant armored airship as troops parachute in
body: Boogie Wings, Data East's 1992 arcade game, "is set around the time of World War I, where the player maneuvers biplanes, automobiles, animals, and various other unidentifiable objects to battle an army of mech-wielding scientists." Among them, "one of the game's bosses is a giant robot Santa Claus" (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Crude Buster
posts.alt: Crude Buster screenshot: a muscle-bound brawler hoisting a road sign in a snowy ruined street
body: In Data East's 1990 brawler, released in the West as Two Crude, "players control Biff and Spike, two mercenaries hired by the American government to stop the terrorist organization Big Valley." "Because the duo are muscle-bound brawlers, they have the ability to pick up objects well beyond their own weight (e.g. cars and traffic lights) to use as weapons." Writing for Computer and Video Games magazine, Julian Rignall called Crude Buster "one of the best beat'em ups I've seen in years" and rated it 95% (per Wikipedia).

posts.title: Air Gallet
posts.alt: Air Gallet screenshot: the flaming demon-statue boss with long tentacle-like hair
body: "When people talk about the fall of Toaplan, four companies always come up: Cave, Raizing, Takumi, and Gazelle," writes Hardcore Gaming 101. Gazelle made only a few games before fading into obscurity, among them Air Gallet, this 1996 vertical shooter published by Banpresto. Of one boss: "It's a large statue of a demon, surrounded in flames, choking others with its long, tentacle-like hair. It has some impressive attacks and starts crying blood once you blow off enough turrets."

posts.title: Pretty Soldier Sailor Moon
posts.alt: Pretty Soldier Sailor Moon screenshot: Sailor Moon leaping over an enemy on a city street
body: "It is the first game to be created by Gazelle, one of the offshoots of defunct developer Toaplan that were founded after they declared bankruptcy in 1994," with "Knuckle Bash designer Junya Inoue serving as one of the game's graphic designers" (per Wikipedia). That May, Japan's Game Machine "listed Pretty Soldier Sailor Moon ... as being the eleventh most-successful arcade game of the month, outperforming titles such as Virtua Fighter."

posts.title: Ikari Warriors
posts.alt: Ikari Warriors screenshot: the soldier advancing up a road toward a squad of enemy troops
body: "Amongst the numerous Commando clones on the market, Ikari Warriors was distinguished by its unique control style with rotary joysticks and a two-player cooperative mode," per Wikipedia. The game "was originally intended to be an official licensed adaptation of the film Rambo: First Blood Part II (1985), but SNK were initially unable to acquire the rights to the film." Even so, "Sylvester Stallone, whose role as John Rambo had an influence on the game, owned an Ikari Warriors arcade cabinet in the 1980s."

posts.title: Fantasy Zone
posts.alt: Fantasy Zone screenshot: Opa-Opa flying through the pastel candy landscape
body: "The main character, Opa-Opa, is sometimes referred to as Sega's first mascot character," and the 1986 game, alongside Konami's TwinBee, is "credited with establishing the cute 'em up subgenre" (per Wikipedia). To arm the little ship, "the player must get money by defeating enemies, bases or bosses, and access a shop by touching a marked balloon."

posts.title: Big Karnak
posts.alt: Big Karnak screenshot: the pharaoh warrior facing a mummy boss among palm trees
body: In Gaelco's 1991 coin-op, per Wikipedia, "players assume the role of a pharaoh warrior who embarks on a journey through four increasingly difficult levels taking place in an Ancient Egypt setting to save his wife by defeating the Egyptian gods Horus, Nut, Isis and Osiris." The studio "decided to work on a horizontal-scrolling arcade game similar to Double Dragon, Bad Dudes Vs. DragonNinja and Ghouls 'n Ghosts." It stayed an arcade exclusive for decades: "The title was never ported to other systems, until its inclusion as part of the Gaelco Arcade 2 compilation for Evercade."

posts.title: Breakout
posts.alt: Breakout arcade screenshot: paddle under the rainbow brick wall
body: "The reason Atari wanted me to design it is they were tired of their games taking 150, 200 chips, and they knew I designed things with very few chips, so we had incentives for getting it under 50 or under 40 chips," Steve Wozniak said of Breakout in a 2007 interview with Gamasutra. Atari's engineers, faced with the finished board: "They had trouble understanding it."

posts.title: Night Slashers
posts.alt: Night Slashers screenshot: a crowd of zombies in the carnival stage
body: "Data East also created Night Slashers, clearly their best beat-em-up and undoubtedly capable of cracking a connoisseur's Top 10 list, if it weren't for the game's unbelievable obscurity," reads a 2006 review at HonestGamers, which sums up the appeal: "watch the heads roll, the chests implode, and the guts soil the cement."

posts.title: Trio The Punch
posts.alt: Trio The Punch screenshot: the giant lucky-cat boss
body: "Nothing about this game is done well, and yet everything just meshes together so perfectly into one gigantic dose of pure nonsense," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, which also says Trio The Punch "is like a visual representation of the writings of William S. Burroughs if he was a Japanese video game designer."

posts.title: Battle Garegga
posts.alt: Battle Garegga screenshot: the stage one bomber under fire
body: "A number of shoot-em-up fans regard it as one of the best shooters of all time; others feel that the cluttered visual style and rank system ruin some pretty cool ideas," writes Hardcore Gaming 101. That rank system reacts to how well you play, and "creates some ridiculous scenarios where players will intentionally suicide at points to make the difficulty manageable, and can only properly clear it by scoring high enough to rack up extra lives."

posts.title: Cabal
posts.alt: Cabal screenshot: the desert stage from the over-the-shoulder view
body: "The rather obscure Japanese TAD Corporation wondered what it would be like to give a third-person point of view to gun-based carnage," writes Hardcore Gaming 101, crediting Cabal with the wave of imitators that followed, Wild Guns and NAM-1975 among them: "None of these games would likely exist if Cabal didn't set the table."

posts.title: Biomechanical Toy
posts.alt: Biomechanical Toy screenshot: the hero riding over a giant blue moon
body: "The colorful, trippy graphics are reminiscent of many Amiga games," writes Hardcore Gaming 101 of Spanish maker Gaelco's run-and-gun. "It's not really on the level of other genre greats like Metal Slug (though it did predate that game by about a year)."

posts.title: Flash Boy
posts.alt: DECO Cassette System loading screen with its countdown counter
body: Flash Boy shipped on Data East's DECO Cassette System, tape-loaded arcade hardware where "the machine was turned on and 2 to 3 minutes later the game was loaded and ready to be played," as AtariProtos describes it. "The tape medium didn't prove to be nearly as robust as Data East had hoped, and the cassettes had a bad habit of failing to load after only a few months."

posts.title: Darius II
posts.alt: Darius II arcade screenshot: the full three-screen panorama
body: "In addition to a three-screen release, there is also a (more common) two-screen version," notes Hardcore Gaming 101; the shot above is the full three-screen spread, while the MiSTer core runs the two-screen one. "For some unknown reason, Darius II was renamed Sagaia outside of Japan."
